name: Aphex Twin
identity: Machines taught to giggle, ambient music with teeth — electronic music's great grinning trickster.

name: Aplete
identity: A creative universe from Las Vegas — songs, art and ideas from multi-instrumentalist Stefano Kajatt.

name: Atoms for Peace
identity: A rhythm section built by a producer, a bass icon and a drummer — assembled to bring Thom Yorke's solo record to life on stage.

name: AURORA
identity: A voice from the Norwegian fjords, singing for nature, warriors, and weirdos alike.

name: Autolux
identity: Noise-pop built for the long game — three albums in two decades, each one a small, exacting world.

name: BADBADNOTGOOD
identity: Jazz chops, hip-hop instincts — a rhythm section that turned crate-digger taste into a live force.

name: Beats Antique
identity: A traveling mechanical circus of Turkish banjo, marching drums, and glitching bass.

name: Béla Fleck and the Flecktones
identity: Banjo-led fusioneers folding bluegrass, jazz, funk, and the future into one band.

name: Björk
identity: Icelandic singer, composer and world-builder, four decades into remaking pop in her own image.

name: Bob Marley
identity: One small island's rhythm, carried to every corner of the earth on a voice that never stopped believing.

name: Bon Iver
identity: A falsetto born in a Wisconsin winter that grew into one of modern music's strangest, warmest bands.

name: Bonobo
identity: Simon Green, the Brighton-born producer who turned downtempo electronica into a full live orchestra of sound.

name: BRAIDS
identity: Slow-building, shimmering art-pop from Montreal — loops and live drums that swell into something enormous.

name: Burial
identity: Rain on a night-bus window, a voice you half-remember — dance music for the hours after the dance.

name: CAN
identity: The Cologne band that built its own studio and invented a rhythm — the most cited influence in experimental rock.

name: Caribou
identity: Songs built like proofs and sung like confessions — electronic music with a heartbeat.

name: Chance the Rapper
identity: Chicago's independent son, who gave his music away free and won Grammys doing it.

name: CocoRosie
identity: Sisters Bianca and Sierra Casady mixing opera, hip-hop, folk and found sounds into a genre of their own making.

name: Coldplay
identity: Arena-scale melancholy built from a UCL dorm-room friendship — the most-attended tour in music history, still touring.

name: Dave Matthews Band
identity: Violin and saxophone where a lead guitar should be — the Charlottesville jam band built for the road, not the record.

name: Dirty Projectors
identity: Art-rock as a moving target — intricate guitar, gymnastic vocal harmony, and a new shape every album.

name: Erykah Badu
identity: A founding voice of neo-soul out of Dallas, still restless and genre-crossing three decades into her catalog.

name: Esperanza Spalding
identity: Bassist, vocalist and composer bending jazz toward chamber music, art-rock and ritual song.

name: Explosions in the Sky
identity: Wordless, cinematic post-rock built from interlocking guitars — songs that swell like weather.

name: Fela Kuti
identity: Ten-minute grooves as political weapons — the bandleader who made Lagos the loudest capital of courage.

name: Flea
identity: The most kinetic bassist in rock, alone at last — trumpet first, voice second, and a covers-and-originals record made under his own name.

name: Flogging Molly
identity: Tin whistle and distortion, built on a Monday-night pub residency — Celtic punk's most enduring band.

name: Florence + the Machine
identity: Cathedral-sized art rock built on Florence Welch's hurricane of a voice.

name: Fred again..
identity: Diary entries built from voice notes and friends' voices, played back to the biggest rooms in dance music.

name: Gorillaz
identity: Damon Albarn and Jamie Hewlett's animated band — pop's longest-running cartoon experiment.

name: Hiatus Kaiyote
identity: Future-soul four-piece bending jazz, funk and electronics into something wholly Melbourne.

name: Hozier
identity: Gospel, blues and Irish folk fused into songs built for both a candlelit room and a stadium singalong.

name: Hundred Waters
identity: Fragile electronics and Nicole Miglis' hushed voice, ringing like a bell from somewhere far off.

name: Immortal Technique
identity: Peruvian-born, Harlem-raised political rap, funded and released entirely outside the major-label system.

name: Imogen Heap
identity: Self-produced electronic pop from a songwriter who builds her own instruments.

name: Iron and Wine
identity: Sam Beam's whispered, bearded folk — home-recorded intimacy that grew into a full-band Americana catalog.

name: Jacob Collier
identity: A one-man harmony lab, discovered on YouTube by Quincy Jones — seven Grammys deep in the art of the arrangement.

name: James Blake
identity: A choirboy's voice suspended in sub-bass — songs built from silence as much as sound.

name: Jason Mraz
identity: The easygoing hitmaker behind 'I'm Yours' — a Billboard chart record and two decades of warm, wordy pop songwriting.

name: Jeff Buckley
identity: A four-octave ache — one finished album and a voice that keeps outliving everything around it.

name: Jesca Hoop
identity: A Californian folk experimentalist weaving strange, luminous songs from her adopted home in Manchester.

name: Joanna Newsom
identity: Harpist, pianist, and singer of long, luminous story-songs from the Sierra foothills.

name: John Frusciante
identity: A guitarist's guitarist chasing every other sound — folk, four-track, prog, and pure electronics — under his own name.

name: Jonny Greenwood
identity: Guitar in one hand, orchestra in the other — the Radiohead multi-instrumentalist who became one of film's defining composers.

name: Jurassic 5
identity: Old-school four-MC trading bars over turntablist production — a deliberate throwback built as a reaction against 90s gangsta rap's dominance.

name: Khruangbin
identity: Thai funk, dub and psych-soul instrumentals from a Houston trio who perform behind wigs, staying anonymous on purpose.

name: Led Zeppelin
identity: Blues turned to thunder — the band that wrote the blueprint for hard rock and heavy metal in four albums flat.

name: Little Dragon
identity: Gothenburg electronic-soul built on a friendship that started in high school — Yukimi Nagano's voice over synths built for both club and headphones.

name: Low Roar
identity: Glacial, aching electronic folk from Reykjavík — the sound of walking a long way home in the cold.

name: Mariachi El Bronx
identity: A hardcore band's second life in charro suits — mariachi played with total sincerity and punk stamina.

name: MF DOOM
identity: The man behind the metal mask — hip-hop's supervillain laureate, 1971–2020. ALL CAPS when you spell the man name.

name: Michael Jackson
identity: Rhythm turned into spectacle — the performer who made the whole planet watch a song.

name: Mimicking Birds
identity: Slow-orbit songs about deep time and small lives — home-recorded intimacy that never got loud on purpose.

name: Modest Mouse
identity: Jagged, restless Pacific Northwest indie rock asking big questions in a cracked voice.

name: Moses Sumney
identity: A weightless falsetto and a refusal of category — avant-soul that makes solitude sound limitless.

name: Nick Drake
identity: A voice like evening light on an English garden — gone at 26, still arriving.

name: Ojos de Brujo
identity: Flamenco palmas over scratched vinyl — a Barcelona collective that wired rumba catalana into hip-hop and kept the copyright for themselves.

name: Oumou Sangaré
identity: The Songbird of Wassoulou, carrying Mali's hunter-society rhythms and women's stories to the world.

name: Perfume Genius
identity: Piano-bench confessions that grew into towering, physical art-pop — beauty and menace in the same breath.

name: Pink Floyd
identity: Albums built like cathedrals — enter anywhere, look up, stay a while.

name: Portishead
identity: Spectral Bristol soul built from crackle, spy-film strings, and a voice that aches in slow motion.

name: Queen
identity: Opera in a rock band's hands — four songwriters and a frontman the stage still misses.

name: Radiohead
identity: Five musicians from Oxfordshire who keep reinventing what a rock band can be.

name: Rage Against the Machine
identity: Rap, funk and metal fused into a weapon — Zack de la Rocha's fury over Tom Morello's turntable-mimicking guitar.

name: Ratatat
identity: Guitars harmonized like synthesizers, synthesizers strutting like guitars — instrumental rock with no singer and no gaps.

name: Red Hot Chili Peppers
identity: Funk-rock's longest conversation — bass, drums, guitar and a frontman locked in for four decades.

name: Santigold
identity: New wave, reggae, punk and pop collapsed into one restless sound, from a Philadelphia songwriter who renamed herself mid-career.

name: Sigur Rós
identity: Icelandic post-rock voyagers who turn glacial landscape into sound.

name: St. Vincent
identity: Annie Clark's art-rock guitar project, built from a touring sideman's apprenticeship into one of the era's defining catalogs.

name: Stevie Wonder
identity: Joy as a musical instrument — played by a man who heard more in the dark than most ever see.

name: Sublime
identity: Long Beach ska, punk and reggae in one sun-warped sound — a catalog that keeps growing decades after it should have stopped.

name: Sufjan Stevens
identity: Whispered epics — a songwriter who orchestrates grief, faith and American myth like nobody else alive.

name: SUUNS
identity: Motorik rock with the wiring exposed — krautrock pulse, post-punk nerves and a sound that never sits still.

name: Tame Impala
identity: Kevin Parker's one-man psychedelic universe from Perth, built alone in the studio and unleashed as a band onstage.

name: The Beatles
identity: Four Liverpudlians who turned eight years of records into the common language of pop.

name: The Books
identity: Found-sound collage as songwriting — cello, guitar and a library of voices nobody else thought to keep.

name: The Boswell Sisters
identity: Three sisters from uptown New Orleans who bent tempo, key, and radio itself to their harmonies.

name: The Smile
identity: Thom Yorke, Jonny Greenwood, and Tom Skinner — Radiohead's restless core rewired through jazz drumming and krautrock nerve.

name: Thievery Corporation
identity: Rob Garza and Eric Hilton's downtempo world-music fusion, run out of their own Washington, D.C. lounge and label for three decades.

name: Thom Yorke
identity: A falsetto threaded through circuitry — Radiohead's voice, wandering alone into the glitch.

name: Tipper
identity: Sound design as its own genre — glitch, bass and downtempo cut with surgical detail and built to move air.

name: Tool
identity: Los Angeles art-metal architects turning odd time signatures into ritual.

name: Tori Amos
identity: Classically trained, confessional and unflinching — piano-driven songwriting that's stayed sharp across three decades.

name: Tortoise
identity: Instrumental music built like architecture — jazz, dub, minimalism and krautrock, played by a band of interchangeable virtuosos.

name: Trust in the Sun
identity: A buttery Las Vegas fusion of jazz, soul, funk and pop.

name: tUnE-yArDs
identity: Loop pedals, ukulele and a voice that builds whole songs from scratch, live — art-pop with the wiring showing.

name: Wye Oak
identity: Two people, a wall of sound — smoky indie rock that keeps shedding its skin.
